content: When consuming starch, salivary amylase begins breaking it down into sugar in the mouth.
Some individuals have higher levels of salivary amylase, leading to incomplete starch breakdown in the mouth.
Incomplete starch breakdown in the mouth causes the body to not recognize carbohydrate intake until digestion has begun.
This unpreparedness during digestion can lead to rapid spikes and crashes in blood sugar levels.
A simple experiment involves comparing the tolerance of sugar and starch by measuring blood glucose responses or logging symptoms.
For the experiment, equal amounts of carbohydrate from sugar (dextrose) and starch (potato starch) are weighed and consumed in random order.
To determine the minimal dose of natural sugar needed to tolerate starch-rich meals, one might add a teaspoon of honey before the meal.
